text: The impact of an AI code generator on learning and retention in introductory programming Background: Researchers have developed several AI code generators to aid novice programmers, such as OpenAI Codex. The ultimate goal of such systems is to help programmers write a large amount of correct code. One concern of using such tools is that they can impede learning and retention of programming skills, which is crucial to the success of introductory programming courses. To investigate this issue, we conducted a controlled study with 69 students aged between 10 and 17 years old from an after-school centre located in Sydney. Background information of the students is shown in Table I. Our study had 69 students working on 45 Python code-authoring tasks. The students were randomly assigned into two groups with the Codex group writing code with the aid of OpenAI Codex and the control group writing code on their own. Students in both groups worked on the same 45 tasks and, after completing all the tasks, worked on 10 code-modification tasks. The results show that students using Codex have a 1.15x increase in authoring tasks and a 1.8x increase in score. The increased use of Codex did not negatively impact students’ ability to complete the modification tasks. Although students who used Codex during training only marginally outperformed those in the control condition in the evaluation post-test held one week later, this is not statistically significant. Students with higher Scratch skills benefited from more retention when they had access to Codex before the post-test.
